html:
II

William the Intruder

вАЬSheвАЩs different from everybody else in the world,вАЭ stammered Robert ecstatically. вАЬYou simply couldnвАЩt describe her. No one could!вАЭ

His mother continued to darn his socks and made no comment.

Only William, his young brother, showed interest.

вАЬHowвАЩs she different from anyone else?вАЭ he demanded. вАЬIs she blind or lame or sumthinвАЩ?вАЭ

Robert turned on him with exasperation.

вАЬOh, go and play at trains!вАЭ he said. вАЬA child like you canвАЩt understand anything.вАЭ

William retired with dignity to the window and listened, with interest unabated, to the rest of the conversation.

вАЬYes, but who is she, dear?вАЭ said their mother. вАЬRobert, I canвАЩt think how you get these big holes in your heels!вАЭ

Robert ran his hands wildly through his hair.

вАЬIвАЩve told you who she is, Mother,вАЭ he said. вАЬIвАЩve been talking about her ever since I came into the room.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I know, dear, but you havenвАЩt mentioned her name or anything about her.вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ Robert spoke with an air of superhuman patience, вАЬsheвАЩs a Miss Cannon and sheвАЩs staying with the Clives and I met her out with Mrs.¬†Clive this morning and she introduced me and sheвАЩs the most beautiful girl IвАЩve ever seen and sheвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Brown hastily, вАЬyou told me all that.вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ went on the infatuated Robert, вАЬwe must have her to tea. I know I canвАЩt marry yetвБ†вАФnot while IвАЩm still at collegeвБ†вАФbut I could get to know her. Not that I suppose sheвАЩd look at me. SheвАЩs miles above meвБ†вАФmiles above anyone. SheвАЩs the most beautiful girl IвАЩve ever seen. You canвАЩt imagine her. You wouldnвАЩt believe me if I described her. No one could describe her. SheвБ†вАФвАЭ

Mrs.¬†Brown interrupted him with haste.

вАЬIвАЩll ask Mrs.¬†Clive to bring her over one afternoon. IвАЩve no more of this blue wool, Robert. I wish you didnвАЩt have your socks such different colours. I shall have to use mauve. ItвАЩs right on the heel; it wonвАЩt show.вАЭ

Robert gave a gasp of horror.

вАЬYou canвАЩt, Mother. How do you know it wonвАЩt show? And even if it didnвАЩt show, the thought of itвБ†вАФ! ItвАЩsвБ†вАФitвАЩs a crisis of my life now IвАЩve met her. I canвАЩt go about feeling ridiculous.вАЭ

вАЬI say,вАЭ said William open-mouthed. вАЬAre you spoony on her?вАЭ

вАЬWilliam, donвАЩt use such vulgar expressions,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Brown. вАЬRobert just feels a friendly interest in her, donвАЩt you, Robert?вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАШA friendly interestвАЩ!вАЭ groaned Robert in despair. вАЬNo one ever tries to understand what I feel. After all IвАЩve told you about her and that sheвАЩs the most beautiful girl IвАЩve ever seen and miles above me and above anyone and you think I feel a вАШfriendly interestвАЩ in her. ItвАЩsвБ†вАФitвАЩs the one great passion of my life! ItвАЩsвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ put in Mrs.¬†Brown mildly, вАЬIвАЩll ring up Mrs.¬†Clive and ask if sheвАЩs doing anything tomorrow afternoon.вАЭ

RobertвАЩs tragic young face lit up, then he stood wrapt in thought, and a cloud of anxiety overcast it.

вАЬEllen can press the trousers of my brown suit tonight, canвАЩt she? And, Mother, could you get me some socks and a tie before tomorrow? Blue, I thinkвБ†вАФa bright blue, you know, not too bright, but not so as you donвАЩt notice them. I wish the laundry was a decent one. You know, a manвАЩs collar ought to shine when itвАЩs new on. They never put a shine on to them. IвАЩd better have some new ones for tomorrow. ItвАЩs so important, how one looks. SheвБ†вАФpeople judge you on how you look. TheyвБ†вАФвАЭ

Mrs.¬†Brown laid her work aside.

вАЬIвАЩll go and ring up Mrs.¬†Clive now,вАЭ she said.

When she returned, William had gone and Robert was standing by the window, his face pale with suspense, and a Napoleonic frown on his brow.

вАЬMrs.¬†Clive canвАЩt come,вАЭ announced Mrs.¬†Brown in her comfortable voice, вАЬbut Miss Cannon will come alone. It appears sheвАЩs met Ethel before. So you neednвАЩt worry any more, dear.вАЭ

Robert gave a sardonic laugh.

вАЬWorry!вАЭ he said, вАЬThereвАЩs plenty to worry about still. What about William?вАЭ

вАЬWell, what about him?вАЭ

вАЬWell, canвАЩt he go away somewhere tomorrow? Things never go right when WilliamвАЩs there. You know they donвАЩt.вАЭ

вАЬThe poor boy must have tea with us, dear. HeвАЩll be very good, IвАЩm sure. Ethel will be home then and sheвАЩll help. IвАЩll tell William not to worry you. IвАЩm sure heвАЩll be good.вАЭ

William had received specific instructions. He was not to come into the house till the tea-bell rang, and he was to go out and play in the garden again directly after tea. He was perfectly willing to obey them. He was thrilled by the thought of Robert in the role of the lovelorn hero. He took the situation quite seriously.

He was in the garden when the visitor came up the drive. He had been told not to obtrude himself upon her notice, so he crept up silently and peered at her through the rhododendron bushes. The proceeding also happened to suit his character of the moment, which was that of a Red Indian chief.

Miss Cannon was certainly pretty. She had brown hair, brown eyes, and dimples that came and went in her rosy cheeks. She was dressed in white and carried a parasol. She walked up the drive, looking neither to right nor left, till a slight movement in the bushes arrested her attention. She turned quickly and saw a small boyвАЩs face, smeared black with burnt cork and framed in hensвАЩ feathers tied on with tape. The dimples peeped out.

вАЬHail, O great chief!вАЭ she said.

William gazed at her open-mouthed. Such intelligence on the part of a grownup was unusual.

вАЬChief Red Hand,вАЭ he supplied with a fierce scowl.

She bowed low, brown eyes alight with merriment.

вАЬAnd what death awaits the poor white face who has fallen defenceless into his hand?вАЭ

вАЬYou better come quiet to my wigwam anвАЩ see,вАЭ said Red Hand darkly.

She threw a glance to the bend in the drive behind which lay the house and with a low laugh followed him through the bushes. From one point the drawing-room window could be seen, and there the anxious Robert stood, pale with anxiety, stiff and upright in his newly-creased trousers (well turned up to show the new blue socks), his soulful eyes fixed steadfastly on the bend in the drive round which the beloved should come. Every now and then his nervous hand wandered up to touch the new tie and gleaming new collar, which was rather too high and too tight for comfort, but which the shopkeeper had informed his harassed customer was the вАЬlatest and most correct shape.вАЭ

Meanwhile the beloved had reached WilliamвАЩs вАЬdugout.вАЭ William had made this himself of branches cut down from the trees and spent many happy hours in it with one or other of his friends.

вАЬHere is the wigwam, Paleface,вАЭ he said in a sepulchral voice. вАЬStand here while I decide with Snake Face and the other chiefs whatвАЩs goinвАЩ to be done to you. ThereвАЩs Snake Face anвАЩ the others,вАЭ he added in his natural voice, pointing to a small cluster of shrubs.

Approaching these, he stood and talked fiercely and unintelligibly for a few minutes, turning his scowling corked face and pointing his finger at her every now and then, as, apparently, he described his capture.

Then he approached her again.

вАЬThat was Red Indian what I was talkinвАЩ then,вАЭ he explained in his ordinary voice, then sinking it to its low, roaring note and scowling more ferociously than ever, вАЬSnake Face says the Paleface must be scalped and cooked and eat!вАЭ

He took out a penknife and opened it as though to perform the operation, then continued, вАЬBut me and the others say that if youвАЩll be a squaw anвАЩ cook for us weвАЩll let you go alive.вАЭ

Miss Cannon dropped on to her knees.

вАЬMost humble and grateful thanks, great Red Hand,вАЭ she said. вАЬI will with pleasure be your squaw.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve gotter fire round here,вАЭ said William proudly, leading her to the back of the wigwam, where a small wood fire smouldered spiritlessly, choked by a large tin full of a dark liquid.

вАЬThat, O Squaw,вАЭ said Red Hand with a dramatic gesture, вАЬis a Paleface we caught lasвАЩ night!вАЭ

The squaw clasped her hands together.

вАЬOh, how lovely!вАЭ she said. вАЬIs he cooking?вАЭ

Red Hand nodded. Then,

вАЬIвАЩll get you some feathers,вАЭ he said obligingly. вАЬYou oughter have feathers, too.вАЭ

He retired into the depth of the wigwam and returned with a handful of hen feathers. Miss Cannon took off her big shady hat and stuck the feathers into her fluffy brown hair with a laugh.

вАЬThis is jolly!вАЭ she said. вАЬI love Red Indians!вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve got some cork you can have to do your face, too,вАЭ went on William with reckless generosity. вАЬIt soon burns in the fire.вАЭ

She threw a glance towards the chimneys of the house that could be seen through the trees and shook her pretty head regretfully.

вАЬIвАЩm afraid IвАЩd better not,вАЭ she said sadly.

вАЬWell,вАЭ he said, вАЬnow IвАЩll go huntinвАЩ and you stir the Paleface and weвАЩll eat him when I come back. Now, IвАЩll be off. You watch me track.вАЭ

He opened his clasp-knife with a bloodthirsty flourish and, casting sinister glances round him, crept upon his hands and knees into the bushes. He circled about, well within his squawвАЩs vision, obviously bent upon impressing her. She stirred the mixture in the tin with a twig and threw him every now and then the admiring glances he so evidently desired.

Soon he returned, carrying over his shoulder a doormat which he threw down at her feet.

вАЬA venison, O squaw,вАЭ he said in a lordly voice. вАЬLet it be cooked. IвАЩve had it out all morning,вАЭ he added in his ordinary tones; вАЬtheyвАЩve not missed it yet.вАЭ

He fetched from the вАЬwigwamвАЭ two small jagged tins and, taking the larger tin off the fire, poured some into each.

вАЬNow,вАЭ he said, вАЬhereвАЩs some Paleface for you, squaw.вАЭ

вАЬOh,вАЭ she said, вАЬIвАЩm sure heвАЩs awfully good, butвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYou neednвАЩt be frightened of it,вАЭ said William protectively. вАЬItвАЩs jolly good, I can tell you.вАЭ He picked up the paper cover of a packet of soup from behind the trees. вАЬItвАЩs jusвАЩ that and water and itвАЩs jolly good!вАЭ

вАЬHow lovely! Do they let youвБ†вАФ?вАЭ

вАЬThey donвАЩt let me,вАЭ he broke in hastily, вАЬbut thereвАЩs heaps in the larder and they donвАЩt notice one every now anвАЩ then. Go on!вАЭ encouragingly, вАЬI donвАЩt mind you having it! Honest, I donвАЩt! IвАЩll get some more soon.вАЭ

Bravely she raised the tin to her lips and took a sip.

вАЬGorgeous!вАЭ she said, shutting her eyes. Then she drained the tin.

WilliamвАЩs face shone with pride and happiness. But it clouded over as the sound of a bell rang out from the house.

вАЬCrumbs! ThatвАЩs tea!вАЭ

Hastily Miss Cannon took the feathers from her hair and put on her hat.

вАЬYou donвАЩt keep a looking-glass in your wigwam I suppose?вАЭ she said.

вАЬN-no,вАЭ admitted William. вАЬBut IвАЩll get one for next time you come. IвАЩll get one from EthelвАЩs room.вАЭ

вАЬWonвАЩt she mind?вАЭ

вАЬShe wonвАЩt know,вАЭ said William simply.

Miss Cannon smoothed down her dress.

вАЬIвАЩm horribly late. What will they think of me? It was awful of me to come with you. IвАЩm always doing awful things. ThatвАЩs a secret between you and me.вАЭ She gave William a smile that dazzled him. вАЬNow come in and weвАЩll confess.вАЭ

вАЬI canвАЩt,вАЭ said William. вАЬIвАЩve got to wash anвАЩ come down tidy. I promised I would. ItвАЩs a special day. Because of Robert, you know. Well you know. Because ofвБ†вАФRobert!вАЭ

He looked up at her mystified face with a significant nod.

Robert was frantic. He had run his hands through his hair so often that it stood around his head like a spiked halo.

вАЬWe canвАЩt begin without her,вАЭ he said. вАЬSheвАЩll think weвАЩre awful. It willвБ†вАФput her off me forever. SheвАЩs not used to being treated like that. SheвАЩs the sort of girl people donвАЩt begin without. SheвАЩs the most beautiful girl IвАЩve ever met in all my life and youвБ†вАФmy own motherвБ†вАФtreat her like this. You may be ruining my life. YouвАЩve no idea what this means to me. If youвАЩd seen her youвАЩd feel more sympathy. I simply canвАЩt describe herвБ†вАФIвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬI said four oвАЩclock, Robert,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Brown firmly, вАЬand itвАЩs after half-past. Ethel, tell Emma she can ring the bell and bring in tea.вАЭ

The perspiration stood out on RobertвАЩs brow.

вАЬItвАЩsвБ†вАФthe downfall of all my hopes,вАЭ he said hoarsely.

Then, a few minutes after the echoes of the tea-bell died away, the front door bell rang sharply. Robert stroked his hair down with wild, unrestrained movements of his hands, and summoned a tortured smile to his lips.

Miss Cannon appeared upon the threshold, bewitching and demure.

вАЬArenвАЩt I perfectly disgraceful?вАЭ she said with her low laugh. вАЬTo tell the truth, I met your little boy in the drive and IвАЩve been with him some time. HeвАЩs a perfect little dear, isnвАЩt he?вАЭ

Her brown eyes rested on Robert. Robert moistened his lips and smiled the tortured smile, but was beyond speech.

вАЬYes, I know Ethel and I met your sonвБ†вАФyesterday, wasnвАЩt it?вАЭ

Robert murmured unintelligibly, raising one hand to the too tight collar, and then bowed vaguely in her direction.

Then they went in to tea.

William, his hair well brushed, the cork partially washed from his face, and the feathers removed, arrived a few minutes later. Conversation was carried on chiefly by Miss Cannon and Ethel. Robert racked his brain for some striking remark, something that would raise him in her esteem far above the ranks of the ordinary young man, but nothing came. Whenever her brown eyes rested on him, however, he summoned the mirthless smile to his lips and raised a hand to relieve the strain of the imprisoning collar. Desperately he felt the precious moments passing and his passion yet unrevealed, except by his eyes, whose message he was afraid she had not read.

As they rose from tea, William turned to his mother, with an anxious sibilant whisper,

вАЬOught I to have put on my best suit too?вАЭ

The demure lights danced in Miss CannonвАЩs eyes and the look the perspiring Robert sent him would have crushed a less bold spirit.

William had quite forgotten the orders he had received to retire from the scene directly after tea. He was impervious to all hints. He followed in the train of the all-conquering Miss Cannon to the drawing-room and sat on the sofa with Robert who had taken his seat next his beloved.

вАЬAre youвБ†вАФerвБ†вАФfond of reading, Miss Cannon?вАЭ began Robert with a painful effort.

вАЬIвБ†вАФwrote a tale once,вАЭ said William boastfully, leaning over Robert before she could answer. вАЬIt was a jolly good one. I showed it to some people. IвАЩll show it to you if you like. It began with a pirate on a raft anвАЩ heвАЩd stole some jewelвАЩry and the king the jewels belonged to was coming after him on a steamer and jusвАЩ when he was cominвАЩ up to him he jumped into the water and took the jewls with him anвАЩ a fish eat the jewls and the king caught it anвАЩ,вАЭ he paused for breath.

вАЬIвАЩd love to read it!вАЭ said Miss Cannon.

Robert turned sideways, and resting an arm on his knee to exclude the persistent William, spoke in a husky voice.

вАЬWhat is your favourite flower, Miss Cannon?вАЭ

WilliamвАЩs small head was craned round RobertвАЩs arm.

вАЬIвАЩve gotter garden. IвАЩve got Virginia Stock growвАЩn all over it. It grows up in no time. AnвАЩ mustвАЩerd вАЩn cress grows in no time, too. I like things what grow quick, donвАЩt you? You get tired of waiting for the other sorts, donвАЩt you?вАЭ

Robert rose desperately.

вАЬWould you care to see the garden and greenhouses, Miss Cannon?вАЭ he said.

вАЬIвАЩd love to,вАЭ said Miss Cannon.

With a threatening glare at William, Robert led the way to the garden. And William, all innocent animation, followed.

вАЬCan you tie knots what canвАЩt come untied?вАЭ he demanded.

вАЬNo,вАЭ she said, вАЬI wish I could.вАЭ

вАЬI can. IвАЩll show you. IвАЩll get a piece of string and show you afterwards. ItвАЩs easy but it wants practice, thatвАЩs all. AnвАЩ IвАЩll teach you how to make aeroplanes out of paper what fly in the air when itвАЩs windy. ThatвАЩs quite easy. Only youвАЩve gotter be careful to get вАЩem the right size. I can make вАЩem and I can make lots of things out of matchboxes anвАЩ things anвАЩвБ†вАФвАЭ

The infuriated Robert interrupted.

вАЬThese are my fatherвАЩs roses. HeвАЩs very proud of them.вАЭ

вАЬTheyвАЩre beautiful.вАЭ

вАЬWell, wait till you see my Virginia Stock! thatвАЩs all. WaitвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬWill you have this tea-rose, Miss Cannon?вАЭ RobertвАЩs face was purple as he presented it. вАЬItвБ†вАФitвБ†вАФerвБ†вАФit suits you. YouвБ†вАФerвБ†вАФflowers and youвБ†вАФthat isвБ†вАФIвАЩm sureвБ†вАФyou love flowersвБ†вАФyou shouldвБ†вАФerвБ†вАФalways have flowers. If IвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬAnвАЩ IвАЩll get you those red ones and that white one,вАЭ broke in the equally infatuated William, determined not to be outshone. вАЬAnвАЩ IвАЩll get you some of my Virginia Stock. AnвАЩ I donвАЩt give my Virginia Stock to anyone,вАЭ he added with emphasis.

When they reentered the drawing-room, Miss Cannon carried a large bouquet of Virginia Stock and white and red roses which completely hid RobertвАЩs tea-rose. William was by her side, chatting airily and confidently. Robert followedвБ†вАФa pale statue of despair.

In answer to RobertвАЩs agonised glance, Mrs.¬†Brown summoned William to her corner, while Robert and Miss Cannon took their seat again upon the sofa.

вАЬI hopeвБ†вАФI hope,вАЭ said Robert soulfully, вАЬI hope your stay here is a long one?вАЭ

вАЬWell, why shanвАЩt I jusвАЩ speak to her?вАЭ WilliamвАЩs whisper was loud and indignant.

вАЬвАКвАЩSh, dear!вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Brown.

вАЬI should like to show you some of the walks around here,вАЭ went on Robert desperately with a fearful glance towards the corner where William stood in righteous indignation before his mother. вАЬIf I could have thatвБ†вАФerвБ†вАФpleasureвБ†вАФerвБ†вАФhonour?вАЭ

вАЬI was only jusвАЩ speaking to her,вАЭ went on WilliamвАЩs voice. вАЬI wasnвАЩt doinвАЩ any harm, was I? Only speaking to her!вАЭ

The silence was intense. Robert, purple, opened his lips to say something, anything to drown that horrible voice, but nothing would come. Miss Cannon was obviously listening to William.

вАЬIs no one else ever to speak to her.вАЭ The sibilant whisper, raised in indignant appeal, filled all the room. вАЬJusвАЩ вАЩcause RobertвАЩs fell in love with her?вАЭ

The horror of the moment haunted RobertвАЩs nights and days for weeks to come.

Mrs.¬†Brown coughed hastily and began to describe at unnecessary length the ravages of the caterpillars upon her husbandвАЩs favourite rose-tree.

William withdrew with dignity to the garden a minute later and Miss Cannon rose from the sofa.

вАЬI must be going, IвАЩm afraid,вАЭ she said with a smile.

Robert, anguished and overpowered, rose slowly.

вАЬYou must come again some time,вАЭ he said weakly but with passion undaunted.

вАЬI will,вАЭ she said. вАЬIвАЩm longing to see more of William. I adore William!вАЭ

They comforted RobertвАЩs wounded feelings as best they could, but it was Ethel who devised the plan that finally cheered him. She suggested a picnic on the following Thursday, which happened to be RobertвАЩs birthday and incidentally the last day of Miss CannonвАЩs visit, and the picnic party was to consist ofвБ†вАФRobert, Ethel, Mrs.¬†Clive and Miss Cannon, and William was not even to be told where it was to be. The invitation was sent that evening and Robert spent the week dreaming of picnic lunches and suggesting impossible dainties of which the cook had never heard. It was not until she threatened to give notice that he reluctantly agreed to leave the arrangements to her. He sent his white flannels (which were perfectly clean) to the laundry with a note attached, hinting darkly at legal proceedings if they were not sent back, spotless, by Thursday morning. He went about with an expression of set and solemn purpose upon his frowning countenance. William he utterly ignored. He bought a book of poems at a secondhand bookshop and kept them on the table by his bed.

They saw nothing of Miss Cannon in the interval, but Thursday dawned bright and clear, and RobertвАЩs anxious spirits rose. He was presented with a watch and chain by his father and with a bicycle by his mother and a tin of toffee (given not without ulterior motive) by William.

They met Mrs.¬†Clive and Miss Cannon at the station and took tickets to a village a few miles away whence they had decided to walk to a shady spot on the river bank.

WilliamвАЩs dignity was slightly offended by his pointed exclusion from the party, but he had resigned himself to it, and spent the first part of the morning in the character of Chief Red Hand among the rhododendron bushes. He had added an ostrich feather found in EthelвАЩs room to his headdress, and used almost a whole cork on his face. He wore the doormat pinned to his shoulders.

After melting some treacle toffee in rainwater over his smoking fire, adding orange juice and drinking the resulting liquid, he tired of the game and wandered upstairs to RobertвАЩs bedroom to inspect his birthday presents. The tin of toffee was on the table by RobertвАЩs bed. William took one or two as a matter of course and began to read the love-poems. He was horrified a few minutes later to see the tin empty, but he fastened the lid with a sigh, wondering if Robert would guess who had eaten them. He was afraid he would. Anyway heвАЩd given him them. And anyway, he hadnвАЩt known he was eating them.

He then went to the dressing-table and tried on the watch and chain at various angles and with various postures. He finally resisted the temptation to wear them for the rest of the morning and replaced them on the dressing-table.

Then he wandered downstairs and round to the shed, where RobertвАЩs new bicycle stood in all its glory. It was shining and spotless and William gazed at it in awe and admiration. He came to the conclusion that he could do it no possible harm by leading it carefully round the house. Encouraged by the fact that Mrs.¬†Brown was out shopping, he walked it round the house several times. He much enjoyed the feeling of importance and possession that it gave him. He felt loth to part with it. He wondered if it was very hard to ride. He had tried to ride one once when he was staying with an aunt. He stood on a garden bench and with difficulty transferred himself from that to the bicycle seat. To his surprise and delight he rode for a few yards before he fell off. He tried again and fell off again. He tried again and rode straight into a holly bush. He forgot everything in his determination to master the art. He tried again and again. He fell off or rode into the holly bush again and again. The shining black paint of the bicycle was scratched, the handle bars were slightly bent and dulled; William himself was bruised and battered but unbeaten.

At last he managed to avoid the fatal magnet of the holly bush, to steer an unsteady ziz-zag course down the drive and out into the road. He had had no particular intention of riding into the road. In fact he was still wearing his befeathered headgear, blacked face, and the mat pinned to his shoulders. It was only when he was actually in the road that he realised that retreat was impossible, that he had no idea how to get off the bicycle.

What followed was to William more like a nightmare than anything else. He saw a motor-lorry coming towards him and in sudden panic turned down a side street and from that into another side street. People came out of their houses to watch him pass. Children booed or cheered him and ran after him in crowds. And William went on and on simply because he could not stop. His iron nerve had failed him. He had not even the presence of mind to fall off. He was quite lost. He had left the town behind him and did not know where he was going. But wherever he went he was the centre of attraction. The strange figure with blackened, streaked face, mat flying behind in the wind and a headdress of feathers from which every now and then one floated away, brought the population to its doors. Some said he had escaped from an asylum, some that he was an advertisement of something. The children were inclined to think he was part of a circus. William himself had passed beyond despair. His face was white and set. His first panic had changed to a dull certainty that this would go on forever. He would never know how to stop. He supposed he would go right across England. He wondered if he were near the sea now. He couldnвАЩt be far off. He wondered if he would ever see his mother and father again. And his feet pedalled mechanically along. They did not reach the pedals at their lowest point; they had to catch them as they came up and send them down with all their might.

It was very tiring; William wondered if people would be sorry if he dropped down dead.

I have said that William did not know where he was going.

But Fate knew.

The picnickers walked down the hill from the little station to the river bank. It was a beautiful morning. Robert, his heart and hopes high, walked beside his goddess, revelling in his nearness to her though he could think of nothing to say to her. But Ethel and Mrs.¬†Clive chattered gaily.

вАЬWeвАЩve given William the slip,вАЭ said Ethel with a laugh. вАЬHeвАЩs no idea where weвАЩve gone even!вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm sorry,вАЭ said Miss Cannon, вАЬIвАЩd have loved William to be here.вАЭ

вАЬYou donвАЩt know him,вАЭ said Ethel fervently.

вАЬWhat a beautiful morning it is!вАЭ murmured Robert, feeling that some remark was due from him. вАЬAm I walking too fast for youвБ†вАФMiss Cannon?вАЭ

вАЬOh, no.вАЭ

вАЬMay I carry your parasol for you?вАЭ he enquired humbly.

вАЬOh, no, thanks.вАЭ

He proposed a boat on the river after lunch, and it appeared that Miss Cannon would love it, but Ethel and Mrs.¬†Clive would rather stay on the bank.

His cup of bliss was full. It would be his opportunity of sealing lifelong friendship with her, of arranging a regular correspondence, and hinting at his ultimate intentions. He must tell her that, of course, while he was at college he was not in a position to offer his heart and hand, but if she could waitвБ†вАФHe began to compose speeches in his mind.

They reached the bank and opened the luncheon baskets. Unhampered by Robert the cook had surpassed herself. They spread the white cloth and took up their position around it under the shade of the trees.

Just as Robert was taking up a plate of sandwiches to hand them with a courteous gesture to Miss Cannon, his eyes fell upon the long, white road leading from the village to the riverside and remained fixed there, his face frozen with horror. The hand that held the plate dropped lifelessly back again on to the tablecloth. Their eyes followed his. A curious figure was cycling along the roadвБ†вАФa figure with blackened face and a few drooping feathers on its head, and a doormat flying in the wind. A crowd of small children ran behind cheering. It was a figure vaguely familiar to them all.

вАЬIt canвАЩt be,вАЭ said Robert hoarsely, passing a hand over his brow.

No one spoke.

It came nearer and nearer. There was no mistaking it.

вАЬWilliam!вАЭ gasped four voices.

William came to the end of the road. He did not turn aside to either of the roads by the riverside. He did not even recognise or look at them. With set, colourless face he rode on to the river bank, and straight amongst them. They fled from before his charge. He rode over the tablecloth, over the sandwiches, patties, rolls and cakes, down the bank and into the river.

They rescued him and the bicycle. Fate was against Robert even there. It was a passing boatman who performed the rescue. William emerged soaked to the skin, utterly exhausted, but feeling vaguely heroic. He was not in the least surprised to see them. He would have been surprised at nothing. And Robert wiped and examined his battered bicycle in impotent fury in the background while Miss Cannon pillowed WilliamвАЩs dripping head on her arm, fed him on hot coffee and sandwiches and called him вАЬMy poor darling Red Hand!вАЭ

She insisted on going home with him. All through the journey she sustained the character of his faithful squaw. Then, leaving a casual invitation to Robert and Ethel to come over to tea, she departed to pack.

Mrs.¬†Brown descended the stairs from WilliamвАЩs room with a tray on which reposed a half-empty bowl of gruel, and met Robert in the hall.

вАЬRobert,вАЭ she remonstrated, вАЬyou really neednвАЩt look so upset.вАЭ

Robert glared at her and laughed a hollow laugh.

вАЬUpset!вАЭ he echoed, outraged by the inadequacy of the expression. вАЬYouвАЩd be upset if your life was ruined. YouвАЩd be upset. IвАЩve a right to be upset.вАЭ

He passed his hand desperately through his already ruffled hair.

вАЬYouвАЩre going there to tea,вАЭ she reminded him.

вАЬYes,вАЭ he said bitterly, вАЬwith other people. Who can talk with other people there? No one can. IвАЩd have talked to her on the river. IвАЩd got heaps of things ready in my mind to say. And William comes along and spoils my whole lifeвБ†вАФand my bicycle. And sheвАЩs the most beautiful girl IвАЩve ever seen in my life. And IвАЩve wanted that bicycle for ever so long and itвАЩs not fit to ride.вАЭ

вАЬBut poor William has caught a very bad chill, dear, so you oughtnвАЩt to feel bitter to him. And heвАЩll have to pay for your bicycle being mended. HeвАЩll have no pocket money till itвАЩs paid for.вАЭ

вАЬYouвАЩd think,вАЭ said Robert with a despairing gesture in the direction of the hall table and apparently addressing it, вАЬyouвАЩd think four grown-up people in a house could keep a boy of WilliamвАЩs age in order, wouldnвАЩt you? YouвАЩd think he wouldnвАЩt be allowed to go about spoiling peopleвАЩs lives andвБ†вАФand ruining their bicycles. Well, he jolly well wonвАЩt do it again,вАЭ he ended darkly.

Mrs.¬†Brown, proceeded in the direction of the kitchen.

вАЬRobert,вАЭ she said soothingly over her shoulder, вАЬyou surely want to be at peace with your little brother, when heвАЩs not well, donвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬPeace?вАЭ he said. Robert turned his haggard countenance upon her as though his ears must have deceived him. вАЬPeace! IвАЩll wait. IвАЩll wait till heвАЩs all right and going about; I wonвАЩt start till then. ButвБ†вАФpeace! ItвАЩs not peace, itвАЩs an armisticeвБ†вАФthatвАЩs all.вАЭ
